summary: Re-verify the REVISED voice/STT hardening: per-session AudioProcessor (WhisperKit) + engineOwner stamp (AppleSpeech)

perLens.round1: All four round-1 findings are CLOSED, verified by interleaving walk against the current working tree. (1) Stale begin unwind/epilogue stripping a successor's tap: closed — every unwind path (taskAccepted-false :253, epilogue :283, install-refusal :212) routes through teardownEngineIfOwner, which no-ops unless engineOwner == that begin's generation; a successor's install sets engineOwner to ITS gen under the same engineLock, so the stale teardown provably no-ops. (2) Route handler re-arming an ownerless engine / double-installing a tap: closed — handleConfigurationChange (:375-394) checks engineOwner == generation AND current-gen INSIDE one engineLock hold, and removeTap-then-install run under that same hold, so a stale notification returns without touching the engine and two taps can never coexist. (3) stopListening killing a successor: closed — the claim is gen-scoped under `lock` and the engine half is ownership-scoped under engineLock; a stop whose begin hasn't armed no-ops on the engine and the begin's own gen re-check inside installTapAsOwner (:297, under engineLock) refuses to arm afterward — I walked all six claim/install/start orderings and none produces the both-no-op running-engine orphan: the engine only runs while some gen owns it, and that gen's claimer always finds owner==gen. (4) WhisperKit cross-session capture teardown: closed structurally — AudioProcessor() builds its OWN AVAudioEngine in setupEngine (AudioProcessor.swift:974-1021, fresh instance, own inputNode tap), stopRecording touches only self.audioEngine, macOS has no shared AVAudioSession state (setupAudioSessionForDevice is compiled out), so a stale stop cannot reach a live successor's capture. The shared lastText/continuation state is generation-guarded at every writer: onState's write+yield are atomic-with-gen-check under lock (:169-175), the stop's capture of lastText is atomic with the gen bump (:241-250), the start-task error path and consumer-cancel are ifGeneration-scoped, and a stale start Task returns before touching the streamer (:214). Question 3's sharper form also holds: installTapAsOwner's removeTap cannot strip a live SUCCESSOR's tap because the caller must be current-gen and ownership only ever moves to a gen that is current at install time — two gens cannot both be current; it CAN strip a superseded predecessor's still-live tap on the shared engine, and that is correct behavior (ownership and the running engine transfer cleanly to the new gen, whose own stop later stops it — walked, no orphan). Question 4: the shared audioEncoder/featureExtractor/segmentSeeker/textDecoder are safe under overlapping streamers — AudioEncoder/FeatureExtractor hold only an MLModel (concurrent prediction is CoreML-supported), TranscribeTask is per-streamer, TextDecoder's per-decode state (DecodingInputs/KV caches) is freshly allocated per run and its EarlyStopActor is keyed by per-window UUID so concurrent runs don't cross-signal.

findings.title: WhisperKit 'named residual' is a PERMANENT hot mic + unbounded memory, not a brief one, and it is reachable on every start (not just first-launch)
findings.interleaving: T1: startListening() → gen=G, streamer S accepted, Task T spawned. T (WhisperKitProvider.swift:214): gen check passes (still G) → await S.startStreamTranscription() → actor S: guard !isRecording OK → await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission() (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:75) — SUSPENDS; this is an XPC hop to TCC on EVERY start (AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission, AudioProcessor.swift:780-782), even when already granted, so the window exists on every VoiceLoop turn/barge-in restart, not only during the first-launch dialog. T1: stopListening() → claims G, gen=G+1, captures S → Task { await S.stopStreamTranscription() } runs on the FREE actor: isRecording=false (already false, no-op), stopRecording() → audioEngine==nil → guard-return (AudioProcessor.swift:1086). Provider now believes the session is dead; continuation finished. T resumes: state.isRecording=true (its didSet fires onState with currentText "" which early-returns on the EMPTY-TEXT guard BEFORE the generation check, WhisperKitProvider.swift:163 — so the guard never sees the staleness) → startRecordingLive() builds a fresh AVAudioEngine, tap installed, engine.start() → MIC HOT → await realtimeLoop() → while state.isRecording spins FOREVER: isRecording=false is set only by stopStreamTranscription, which no path ever calls again for S (provider's streamer ref was nilled; a later public stopListening claims the CURRENT gen and stops the CURRENT streamer; S is retained alive by its own running loop so AudioProcessor.deinit never fires). Consequences beyond 'brief self-hot-mic': (1) mic indicator on until app quit — in a privacy-first product; (2) AudioStreamTranscriber never calls purgeAudioSamples, so the zombie's audioSamples grows unbounded (~64 KB/s ≈ 225 MB/h) and each transcribeCurrentBuffer copies+decodes the ENTIRE growing buffer (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:125,159), compounding CPU/ANE contention against the live session's shared audioEncoder/textDecoder; (3) even realtimeLoop's error-exit (break at :102) leaves the engine recording — the loop exit does not stopRecording. Scoping IS correct: only S's own processor/engine is affected, cross-session capture damage is impossible — but 'brief' and 'first-launch-only' are both wrong.
findings.suggested_fix: Give the zombie a self-kill seam, mirroring AppleSpeech's commit-point epilogue: hold the streamer in a small reference box set immediately after construction, and move the generation check to the TOP of onState (before the empty-text guard); on staleness, fire Task { await box.value?.stopStreamTranscription() } once. Because state.isRecording=true fires the didSet callback synchronously BEFORE startRecordingLive, and the actor suspends at realtimeLoop's first await, the scheduled stop lands right after capture starts: isRecording=false + stopRecording() → loop exits → zombie dead within ~one hop, bounding the residual to milliseconds of capture. Optionally also pre-flight await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission() in the provider's Task BEFORE the generation re-check at :214, shrinking the primary window. Update the header's residual note either way — the current acceptance rationale ('brief', 'first-launch mic-permission resolution') is factually wrong on both counts.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: engineLock self-deadlock if AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange is ever posted synchronously on the thread inside engine.start()/stop()
findings.interleaving: handleConfigurationChange (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:377) holds non-recursive engineLock while calling audioEngine.stop()/start(); the observer is registered with queue: nil (:352), so its handler runs on the POSTING thread. If AVFoundation ever posts .AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange synchronously from within start() or stop() on the calling thread (e.g. a format re-evaluation during the route-change restart), the handler re-enters engineLock.withLock on the same thread → self-deadlock. Empirically the notification is delivered asynchronously from the engine's internal thread — I could not construct a concrete trigger, so this is a hardening note, not a proven defect. Same exposure exists for teardownEngineIfOwner/startEngineIfOwner.
findings.suggested_fix: Cheap immunization: have the notification closure dispatch handleConfigurationChange onto a private serial DispatchQueue (or a detached Task) instead of running it inline on the posting thread. This also removes any dependence on Apple's undocumented posting-thread behavior across OS versions.

findings.title: Upstream WhisperKit: AudioProcessor.audioSamples is read on the streamer actor while appended on the tap thread (pre-existing, unchanged by this diff)
findings.interleaving: AudioProcessor.processBuffer (AudioProcessor.swift:908) appends to the plain ContiguousArray audioSamples on the tap-callback thread, while AudioStreamTranscriber.transcribeCurrentBuffer (:125) reads audioProcessor.audioSamples on the actor with no synchronization — a data race that can crash on array reallocation. This existed identically with the shared kit.audioProcessor, so the per-session revision neither introduced nor widened it; noting for completeness because the revision's correctness now rests entirely on WhisperKit internals. Related benign sibling: TextDecoder.languageLogitsFilter (TextDecoder.swift:493,634-639) is a lazily-cached non-atomic optional — only reachable via detectLanguage, which the provider avoids by pinning language "en" for .en variants; a future multilingual variant would expose a torn-write hazard under concurrent decodes.
findings.suggested_fix: No in-repo action; track as an upstream WhisperKit issue (wrap audioSamples access in a lock or make AudioProcessor an actor). If a multilingual variant is ever adopted, re-verify the detectLanguage path under session overlap.

perLens.onFaith: (1) AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange is posted asynchronously (never synchronously on the thread executing engine.start()/stop() under engineLock) — believed from observed AVFoundation behavior, not provable from source; if Apple ever posts it inline, the queue:nil observer + non-recursive NSLock self-deadlocks (filed as nit hardening). (2) CoreML MLModel.prediction is thread-safe under concurrent calls from two streamers — Apple-documented, not re-proven here. (3) audioEngine.stop()/removeTap drain in-flight tap callbacks but do not call out into arbitrary code holding other locks, and engine.start() does not block for the mic TCC dialog while engineLock is held (if it did: a seconds-long stall of a concurrent stopListening, not a deadlock). (4) Two AVAudioEngine capture instances running concurrently on macOS during a session overlap are HAL-supported (separate IO procs) — consistent with the per-session design working at all, but verify-by-launch per this repo's convention. (5) SFSpeechAudioBufferRecognitionRequest tolerates a few buffers at a superseded route's format during the tap-handoff window (pre-existing behavior, unchanged). All mic/TCC-path conclusions are static analysis per the task's premise that these paths cannot run under swift test.

perLens.round1: All five round-1 blockers are CLOSED in the revised code. AppleSpeechTranscriber: (1) stale begin unwind/epilogue stripping a successor's tap — closed: unwind (line 253), commit-point epilogue (283), and every error path route through teardownEngineIfOwner, which no-ops unless engineOwner == that generation under engineLock; walked the stop-between-start-and-epilogue interleaving both orders — exactly one of {stop's teardown, epilogue's teardown} fires, and 'both no-op' provably requires the engine to be already-down or successor-owned. (2) route handler re-arming an ownerless engine or double-installing a tap — closed: handleConfigurationChange checks engineOwner == generation AND gen-currency inside the same engineLock hold as the remove/reinstall, so an ownerless engine no-ops and a mid-install successor is serialized behind the lock (owner flips before the handler can see it). (3) stopListening killing a successor — closed: the claim is atomic under lock and teardownEngineIfOwner(claimedGeneration) only acts on ownership match; a successor that armed in the gap holds ownership and is untouched. Also verified the predecessor-handover chain (stop claims G, successor installs before the stop's teardown runs): the stop's teardown no-ops on the ownership check, the successor correctly inherits/starts the engine, and the degenerate-format install-failure branch recovers via the predecessor's still-owed synchronous teardown — no permanently-leaked owner found on this class. WhisperKitProvider: (4) cross-session capture teardown — closed structurally: AudioProcessor.audioEngine is instance state (AudioProcessor.swift:206), setupEngine builds a fresh AVAudioEngine per session (975), stopRecording touches only self.audioEngine (1085-1105); no shared session object on macOS. (5) shared lastText/continuation pollution — closed: onState's lastText write and currency check are one lock hold; stop's claim captures streamer/continuation/lastText atomically and bumps generation; the start-task error path and consumer-cancel are generation-scoped. Lock-ordering also proven: engineLock→lock is the only nesting direction (installTapAsOwner:297, handleConfigurationChange:380); every lock-only body (95, 122, 193, 240, 281, 345, 359) never touches engineLock; the tap callback takes only lock, so audioEngine.stop() blocking on in-flight taps under engineLock cannot cycle.

findings.title: WhisperKit: a stop landing inside startStreamTranscription's permission suspension leaves that session's engine hot FOREVER, not 'briefly' — the stop consumed the only reference, so no teardown path ever exists again
findings.interleaving: WhisperKitProvider.swift: (1) startListening → gen=G, streamer accepted, Task passes the gen check at line 214 and awaits streamer.startStreamTranscription(). (2) Inside the actor (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:73-87): `guard !state.isRecording` passes, then it SUSPENDS at `await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()` — the actor is now free. On first launch this window is the whole TCC dialog (seconds–minutes); even with permission granted it is a real actor-hop window. (3) stopListening() (silence endpoint, user cancel, or restart) claims G → gen=G+1, captures the streamer, and fire-and-forgets `Task { await stopped?.stopStreamTranscription() }` (line 252). (4) That teardown runs on the free actor NOW: `state.isRecording = false` (already false), `audioProcessor.stopRecording()` → `guard let engine = audioEngine else { return }` (AudioProcessor.swift:1086) — engine is nil, TOTAL NO-OP. (5) The permission await resumes: `state.isRecording = true`, `startRecordingLive` builds+starts the engine — MIC HOT — and `realtimeLoop()` spins while isRecording. (6) Nothing ever calls stopStreamTranscription on this streamer again: the provider's `streamer` slot was nil'd by the claim, the continuation is finished, all onState callbacks are gen-stale and dropped, and startStreamTranscription never returns (it awaits realtimeLoop). Consequences beyond the hot mic: `audioSamples` is never purged by AudioStreamTranscriber, so the ContiguousArray grows unboundedly (~230 MB/hour at 16 kHz Float32), and the zombie's realtimeLoop keeps driving the SHARED audioEncoder/textDecoder every ~1 s of new audio, contending with every live session's decodes until app quit. It IS correctly scoped to one session's processor (cross-session damage impossible), but the header's 'running briefly … ⌘R-owed' framing is wrong: the accepted-risk analysis rested on a false duration premise. Realistic trigger: first-run voice mode → TCC dialog up → voice loop times out / user cancels (stop fires) → user then clicks Allow → permanent invisible hot mic in a privacy-first product.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the stop able to reach the streamer AFTER its start commits. Concretely: retain the start Task handle in provider state alongside the streamer; in stopListening's claimed path run `startTask.cancel(); _ = await startTask.value; await stopped?.stopStreamTranscription()` in the fire-and-forget Task. Cancellation makes realtimeLoop's Task.sleep throw → loop breaks → startStreamTranscription returns → the SECOND stopStreamTranscription now sees the live engine and kills it; if the start never got past permission, the awaited value returns with no engine and the second stop no-ops. Optionally also pre-flight `await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()` in the provider's start Task BEFORE the generation re-check, so the wide TCC window sits before the commit check instead of after it. At minimum, correct the header's residual note from 'briefly' to 'until app quit' so the accepted risk is honestly stated.

findings.title: WhisperKit: a racing onState can yield a duplicate partial segment AFTER the stop's final segment (before finish)
findings.interleaving: onState checks generation and writes lastText under lock (WhisperKitProvider.swift:169-173) but yields OUTSIDE the lock (175). Interleaving: onState passes the gen check → stopListening claims G, yields TranscriptSegment(finalText, isFinal: true) and is about to finish() → the raced onState's `continuation.yield(text, isFinal: false)` lands between the final yield and finish. The consumer sees final=true followed by a partial of the same (or byte-identical) cumulative text, then finish. Yield-after-finish is safely dropped by AsyncStream; only the between-final-and-finish window is reachable. Harmless under TranscriptAccumulator's latest-text-wins fold since the text is identical, but any downstream that treats isFinal as a strict stream terminator would see an anomalous trailing partial.
findings.suggested_fix: Either document the tolerated ordering at the yield site, or have onState capture a 'still current' token and yield while still inside the lock's critical decision (e.g., return the continuation to yield from within the same claimed check), or have downstream ignore non-final segments after a final one.

findings.title: WhisperKit shared TextDecoder.languageLogitsFilter is an unsynchronized lazy cache — a latent data race if two streamers ever decode concurrently with language == nil (dormant today: .en models pin language)
findings.interleaving: TextDecoder.swift:634-639 does `let f = self.languageLogitsFilter ?? LanguageLogitsFilter(...); self.languageLogitsFilter = f` with no synchronization. The provider deliberately overlaps a stopping streamer's in-flight decode with the successor's first decode (stop is fire-and-forget), and the zombie in finding 1 makes the overlap unbounded. Both TranscribeTasks share the one TextDecoder. With the current `base.en` default, `language: "en"` skips detection so the path never executes — but a future multilingual variant (the DecodingOptions comment explicitly anticipates `nil` → auto-detect) would race two threads on this write. Practically both write equivalent filters and a reference store won't tear, but it is UB under Swift's memory model and TSan-visible.
findings.suggested_fix: No code change needed now. Add a one-line caveat next to the DecodingOptions `language:` derivation in WhisperKitProvider.swift: a multilingual (non-.en) variant re-opens a shared-TextDecoder lazy-cache race in WhisperKit's language detection — gate that variant switch on upstreaming a fix or serializing streamer overlap.

findings.title: AppleSpeech: config-change observer uses queue: nil, so the handler runs synchronously on the posting thread — if AVAudioEngine ever posts .AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange synchronously from inside stop()/start() on a thread already holding engineLock, the non-reentrant NSLock self-deadlocks
findings.interleaving: teardownEngineIfOwner / startEngineIfOwner / handleConfigurationChange hold engineLock across audioEngine.stop()/start() (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:152-157, 311-322, 377-393). NotificationCenter.addObserver(queue: nil) (line 351-355) delivers on the POSTING thread. If any of those engine calls posted the configuration-change notification synchronously on the calling thread, handleConfigurationChange would re-enter engineLock.withLock → NSLock is non-reentrant → self-deadlock. Empirically AVAudioEngine posts this notification from an internal thread (asynchronously), so no cycle exists today — but that is framework behavior taken on faith, not provable from this source.
findings.suggested_fix: Defensive hardening: pass an OperationQueue (e.g., a dedicated serial queue or .main) to addObserver so the handler is never synchronous with the poster, or hop the handler body onto a Task before taking engineLock. Zero behavior change in the normal path; removes the faith dependency.

perLens.onFaith: (1) These paths cannot run under swift test, so every conclusion is static reasoning, not execution — per the task's premise. (2) AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange is assumed to be posted asynchronously from an internal framework thread, never synchronously from within stop()/start() on the caller's thread — the nit-4 self-deadlock is unreachable only if that holds (matches observed Apple behavior; not provable from source). (3) MLModel.prediction is thread-safe for concurrent calls on one instance (Apple-documented) — load-bearing for the shared audioEncoder/textDecoder overlap verdict. (4) Multiple concurrent AVAudioEngine instances can each run an input tap on macOS via independent HAL IOProcs — load-bearing for 'fresh AudioProcessor = truly independent'; standard behavior, unverified here. (5) SFSpeechAudioBufferRecognitionRequest.append is safe to call from the render thread under our lock, and AVAudioEngine.start() on an already-running engine is a harmless no-op — both assumed per framework convention. (6) Actor job scheduling: I assume no fairness guarantee between the stop's fire-and-forget teardown and the start's actor hop, which is what makes the blocking finding's no-suspension variant reachable too; the permission-suspension variant does not depend on this assumption.

perLens.round1: All four round-1 findings verified closed against the working tree. (1) WhisperKit cross-session capture teardown: structurally impossible now — each AudioStreamTranscriber gets its own AudioProcessor() (WhisperKitProvider.swift:188); AudioProcessor's engine is a per-instance property built in startRecordingLive/setupEngine with no shared mutable statics, and stopRecording touches only self.audioEngine; on macOS there is no shared AVAudioSession, and multiple engines capturing the same device is supported. A stale session cannot damage a live one's capture. (2) AppleSpeech stale begin unwind/epilogue stripping a successor's tap: closed — the unwind (AppleSpeechTranscriber.swift:253) and the commit-point epilogue (:283) go through teardownEngineIfOwner(G), which no-ops unless engineOwner==G under engineLock; a successor that armed holds owner==G_new, so the stale paths cannot touch it. (3) Route handler re-arming an ownerless engine / double-tap crash: closed — handleConfigurationChange(generation:) requires BOTH engineOwner==generation and generation current, checked inside the same engineLock hold as the removeTap/reinstall (:377-386); a notification from a removed observer for a stopped session fails the owner check; a second-tap collision is impossible because install paths removeTap first within the same engineLock hold and are serialized on engineLock. (4) stopListening killing a successor: closed — the claim is generation-gated under lock and the engine pass is teardownEngineIfOwner(claimedGeneration) (:142), so a stop for a handed-on session no-ops at the ownership check. I also verified the 'both no-op, engine left running' hunt fails: for a stop's teardown and the epilogue's teardown to BOTH no-op, engineOwner must have moved off G, which only a successor's installTapAsOwner does — and that successor then owns the (running) engine with all its own teardown paths intact; every other interleaving leaves exactly one of the two guarded teardowns succeeding. WhisperKit shared transcript state: lastText write and yield are generation-gated under lock (:169-175); a stale start-task error path calls stopListening(ifGeneration:) which no-ops without finishing a successor's continuation; orphaned continuations from racing startListening calls are always finished by their own begin/accepted-guard exit paths.

findings.title: WhisperKit residual is a PERMANENT ownerless hot mic + unbounded memory growth, not a 'brief self-hot-mic' — and it does not need the TCC dialog
findings.interleaving: Session G: startListening builds streamer, stores it (accepted), spawns start Task. Start Task passes its generation check (Sources/M1K3WhisperKit/WhisperKitProvider.swift:214) and calls streamer.startStreamTranscription() — an actor-job enqueue. User (or router restart / consumer cancel) calls stopListening(): claims G, bumps to G+1, fire-and-forgets Task { stopped.stopStreamTranscription() } (WhisperKitProvider.swift:252). Actor job order is unspecified: if the STOP job runs first, stopStreamTranscription sets isRecording=false (already false) and audioProcessor.stopRecording() no-ops on a nil engine (AudioProcessor.swift:1086 'guard let engine = audioEngine else { return }'). Then the START job runs: 'guard !state.isRecording' passes, isRecording=true, startRecordingLive() arms this session's engine, realtimeLoop() spins while isRecording — FOREVER. The identical outcome occurs via the named residual: start job suspends at 'await AudioProcessor.requestRecordPermission()' (AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:75), actor reentrancy lets the stop job run its no-op, then the start resumes and commits. In both variants: provider.streamer is already nil, the one-shot stop was spent, the Task→realtimeLoop retains streamer→TranscribeTask→AudioProcessor so deinit-stopRecording can never fire. Result: mic recording indefinitely after the user stopped (orange indicator lit, privacy-core product), audioSamples never purged (AudioStreamTranscriber never calls purgeAudioSamples — ~64 KB/s ≈ 230 MB/h growth), and realtimeLoop re-decodes the ENTIRE growing buffer every pass on the SHARED audioEncoder/textDecoder, permanently degrading the live successor's decode latency. Transcript state stays clean (onState generation guard holds) and the successor's own capture is untouched — but the consequence is materially worse than the header's 'briefly hot', and the second (no-TCC, actor-hop) window means it is not confined to first-launch permission resolution.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the stop sticky by re-checking at the actor-side commit point, which is observable: state.isRecording flipping false→true fires stateChangeCallback synchronously on the actor (didSet at AudioStreamTranscriber.swift:24-27, set at :79 BEFORE startRecordingLive). In onState, before the text guards, add: if !oldState.isRecording && newState.isRecording, and lock.withLock({ generation != self.generation }), then fire Task { await streamer.stopStreamTranscription() } — that job serializes after the in-flight startStreamTranscription's synchronous section, flips isRecording false, stops the engine, and lets realtimeLoop exit (which also releases the retain cycle). Plumb the streamer into the closure via a small weak-box populated immediately after the AudioStreamTranscriber is constructed (callbacks cannot fire before startStreamTranscription runs, which is after the box is set). The onState signature already receives oldState as the currently ignored first parameter. Also correct the header Review note: the residual is any stop landing between the start-task's generation check and the actor executing the commit — not only inside the permission suspension — and without the fix it is permanent, not brief.

findings.title: Latent unsynchronized lazy-init data race on TextDecoder.languageLogitsFilter if a multilingual (non-.en) Whisper variant ever ships
findings.interleaving: Overlapping teardown: old streamer's in-flight transcribeCurrentBuffer completes one final decode after stopStreamTranscription (isRecording checked only at loop top) while the new session's first decode starts. Both TranscribeTasks share kit.textDecoder. For a model without the .en suffix the provider passes language:nil (WhisperKitProvider.swift:195) and TranscribeTask.run calls textDecoder.detectLanguage when isModelMultilingual (TranscribeTask.swift:312), which does an unlocked read-then-write of the instance var self.languageLogitsFilter (TextDecoder.swift:634-639) — a class-reference var written from two threads concurrently: benign-looking lazy-init, but a real data race under TSan/Swift memory model. Unreachable today: base.en (default) pins language 'en' and isModelMultilingual is false, so detectLanguage never runs. All other shared components verified stateless per-call (prepareDecoderInputs allocates fresh KV arrays per invocation; SegmentSeeker/AudioEncoder/FeatureExtractor hold only the MLModel, whose prediction API is thread-safe).
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the WhisperKitProvider Review note / MODEL_CHOICES guard: 'multilingual variants (no .en suffix) drive TextDecoder.detectLanguage, whose languageLogitsFilter lazy-init is not thread-safe under session-overlap — serialize sessions or pin language before adopting one.' No code change owed while only .en variants ship.

perLens.onFaith: Taken on faith, not proven here: (1) MLModel.prediction thread-safety under concurrent invocation from two TranscribeTasks — Apple-documented behavior, relied on for the transient-overlap safety of the shared audioEncoder/featureExtractor/textDecoder; a CoreML regression here would surface as crashes during the seconds-long teardown overlap, and cannot be exercised under swift test. (2) AVAudioEngineConfigurationChange is never posted SYNCHRONOUSLY on the thread calling engine.start()/stop() — if it ever were, handleConfigurationChange would re-enter the non-recursive engineLock on the same thread (self-deadlock); empirically these notifications arrive on a HAL/media-services listener thread, and this structure (engine ops under engineLock) pre-dates this revision, but it is unproven. (3) Multiple concurrent AVAudioEngine instances capturing the same macOS input device is safe — established platform practice, not spec-guaranteed. (4) Swift actor job scheduling: I assumed NO FIFO ordering between the start task's actor job and the stop task's (adversarial worst case); the blocking finding holds under ANY ordering assumption because the permission-suspension reentrancy variant produces the same zombie even if enqueue order were preserved. (5) SFSpeechAudioBufferRecognitionRequest.append tolerating a transient format/route mismatch during handoff windows — Apple framework behavior, verify-by-launch per the file's own convention.
